text: The surgeon begins the procedure by removing fat from the anterior abdominal wall to ensure the mesh sits flat against the posterior sheath. The falciform ligament is visible, and the setup includes three eight-millimeter trocars on the right side.

The surgeon removes midline fat from the anterior abdominal wall, exposing the glistening white posterior sheath above. The dissection progresses towards the umbilicus, ensuring the mesh will sit flat against the fascia without interference from fat.

The surgeon divides the umbilical ligaments to access and remove hypogastric fat, revealing the stoma with visible prolapse. Adhesions around the stoma site are carefully dissected to allow full retraction of the bowel from the stoma aperture, ensuring no redundant bowel remains inside the mesh. The dissection is performed circumferentially, with caution to avoid injury to the bowel or mesentery.
The surgeon retracts the stoma into the intraperitoneal location by a few centimeters, carefully avoiding excessive use of cautery to prevent thermal spread.

The surgeon reduces the intra-abdominal pressure to about eight, creating less space in the surgical field. They proceed to close a two-centimeter aperture, ensuring not to incorporate the bowel or mesentery while holding them out of the way. Care is taken to avoid making the aperture too tight.

The surgeon passes a large bore Foley catheter through the fascia without tension and proceeds to lateralize the stoma. The bowel is positioned laterally, and bites of mesenteric and epiploic fat are taken to wrap around the bowel, ensuring the mesh contacts only the fat and not the bowel wall.

The surgeon lateralizes the bowel on the opposite side, creating a lateralization of approximately seven to eight centimeters. A 20 by 15 coated mesh is positioned with the non-coated side facing the anterior abdominal wall, eliminating the use of trans-fascial sutures.

The surgeon begins by placing a stitch at the center midline of the mesh. Another suture is then placed on the inferior aspect of the stoma, directly opposite the original stitch. The surgeon sews along the inferior aspect of the lateralized stoma, creating a tunnel for the stoma within the mesh.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia site, ensuring it lays flat by suturing around the edges. Barbed sutures are used for secure placement, starting from the twelve o'clock position and sewing superiorly. The surgeon uses eighteen-inch sutures for efficiency, despite their cumbersome nature in the initial throws.

The surgeon uses remaining sutures to bring the surrounding fat up to the mesh, covering it and preventing any loose tissue from dangling. This action may facilitate quicker peritonealization of the mesh.
